ai_assist.reasoning_note: Extract the sensor's maximum rated tracking acceleration in G/gravities, usually shown as "Max Acceleration (G)", "up to 50G acceleration", or a sensor datasheet "Acceleration" row. Do not read grams/weight values such as "54g" as acceleration. If a component datasheet lists multiple operating modes, use the highest gaming/high-performance/corded value under the upper_bound policy, not low-power or office-mode values. Prefer exact product official specs; otherwise use the resolved exact sensor's maker/authorized distributor datasheet or official sensor technology page. Product-level official specs override a generic sensor row. ai_assist.reasoning_note: Extract the left/right main-button click or actuation force in gf. Prefer an exact product-level value for the requested model/variant (brand spec, lab measurement, or review measurement) over a bare switch spec. If only the switch model is proven, use the switch component's nominal operating force as the product value; when PRODUCT_COMPONENTS did not already prove the switch, cite both the product switch-identity source and the switch-force source. Treat "operating force", "actuation force", "tactile force", "button force", "press force", "click weight", and "grams of force" as aliases for this field. Do not confuse this with release/releasing force, switch lifespan/click count, click latency/debounce, actuation distance/pre-travel/post-travel, or L/R force deviation. Normalize tolerance values to the nominal center: 65±10gf -> 65. Convert Newtons to gf using N×101.97 and round to the nearest whole gf: 0.59 N -> 60. If separate left/right values are given, use their average unless the source gives a combined value. If only a binned range is marketed, use the midpoint rounded to the nearest gf. For hot-swap mice or editions/batches with different installed switches, use only the exact/default factory-installed switch for the requested variant; return unk if the installed switch/variant cannot be pinned down. Product-level measurements may override component nominal force because shell/button implementation changes real click force.

Return `hybrid` only when evidence explicitly supports both a wired USB data mode and at least one wireless computer connection mode; phrases like "tri-mode," "wired + wireless," "wireless/wired," or a spec list containing both "Wired" and wireless protocols qualify. Prefer detailed tech specs, manuals, or support setup guides over marketing titles and aggregator summaries. Treat "zero debounce", "no debounce delay", and "doesn't require debouncing" as 0 ms only when stated for the exact product or for the exact switch technology the product uses; do not use a nearby switch actuation value, such as 0.2 ms actuation, as debounce. Accept labels such as debounce time, debounce delay, debounce rate, click debounce, key response time, button response time, and click response time only when the source gives numeric ms values or explicitly maps the label to debounce. ai_assist.reasoning_note: Extract the product's LOD setting capability/behavior, not the numeric lift-off distance itself. Return `adjustable` when a credible source says LOD/lift-off distance/tracking distance/lift-off range can be changed in software, by button combinations, by onboard/OLED controls, or lists multiple user-selectable LOD heights. Return `surface-tuned` when a credible source says the mouse supports surface calibration/tuning, mouse-mat calibration, Smart Tracking, automatic surface calibration, or mouse-feet-height tracking optimization; this may co-occur with `adjustable`. Return `fixed low` or `fixed high` only when no credible exact-product source supports user-selectable LOD and a credible spec/review/lab source positively proves the fixed behavior: low if the source calls it low/very low or the numeric LOD is <=2.4 mm; high if the source calls it high/too high or the numeric LOD is >=3.6 mm. Do not infer fixed just because the product page omits LOD settings, and do not emit `low`, `medium`, `high`, `1 mm`, `2 mm`, etc. as list items. If only a numeric fixed LOD falls between the low/high thresholds, if adjustability cannot be ruled in or out, or if the evidence is only generic to a sensor/software family without exact-product support, return `unk` for this field and let `lift` carry the numeric value. Treat optional firmware/mode changes that remove LOD adjustment as edition/mode-specific only when the requested product identity ships or is explicitly requested in that mode; otherwise use the stock/default product capability.

ai_assist.reasoning_note: Extract supported polling/report/USB report rates in Hz for the exact mouse and exact variant/receiver bundle. Return every explicitly selectable rate for the exact SKU/mode when a source lists them. If sources only prove "up to" or "maximum" polling rate, return the proven maximum only; do not invent standard lower steps. Optional dongles, receivers, firmware upgrades, or wired/wireless/Bluetooth limits count only when the source says the exact product supports them; record the condition in evidence/log notes. Prefer explicit labels such as "programmable buttons," "programmable controls," "programmable inputs," "macro programmable buttons," "fully configurable buttons," or "customizable buttons." Normalize arithmetic marketing counts (for example, 10+1 -> 11 and 19+1 -> 20). If a source distinguishes "buttons" from broader "controls," use the count attached to the programmable-buttons label; use a programmable-controls/input count only when that is the clearest available label for remappable mouse inputs. For mice with included swappable side plates or layouts, use the highest count available in the retail configuration when the source says "up to" and the parts are included. Do not count power, pairing, receiver-storage, wheel-mode toggle, or other controls explicitly marked not programmable. Do not infer a value from visible buttons, side-button count, or total physical button count alone; return unk when sources only show physical button count or generic software customizability without a count.

If the source names a proprietary or rebadged family such as Logitech HERO, Razer Focus/Focus Pro/Focus X, SteelSeries TrueMove, Corsair MARKSMAN, or Microsoft BlueTrack, return the family owner as sensor_brand even when PixArt or another partner is mentioned as co-designer/OEM; this field is not the hidden silicon-OEM field. ai_assist.reasoning_note: Extract the announcement, launch, first-public availability, or first-supported date of the resolved sensor component, not the mouse product release date. Prefer exact sensor-level maker pages, datasheets, official technology pages, brand press releases, support/software release notes, or official launch posts. If a dated source says the sensor is "new", "first-ever", "debuting", "revealed", "introduced", "all-new", or "first to feature" in that product, the source's publication/release date may be used as sensor_date. Do not use an ordinary product release date, retailer "Date first available", review publication date, or mouse launch date unless the text explicitly ties that date to the sensor's introduction or availability. Normalize exact dates as `YYYY-MM-DD`; when only month+year is proven, use `YYYY-MM-01` only if the project accepts month-level dates and note month-level precision in evidence; do not invent a day from year-only evidence. Sensor date usually predates the product release, but can post-date it for firmware-upgraded sensors or later renamed/tuned generations, so do not enforce `sensor_date <= release_date` as a hard rule.

ai_assist.reasoning_note: Extract a numeric movement/sensor latency only when the source reports a measured time for mouse movement delay, such as RTINGS "Delay At Half Movement" or a source that explicitly defines movement/shift latency as delay between physical mouse movement and computer/USB detection. Prefer RTINGS Delay At Half Movement for the scalar value under the current RTINGS bench; do not convert RTINGS scores or qualitative phrases like "outstanding sensor latency" into ms. MouseSpecs "Shift Latency" may be used only as a tier5 fallback when the page definition is movement latency; do not confuse it with DPI-shift button latency. Reject click latency, switch actuation/debounce delay, polling-interval math, receiver/wireless latency, NVIDIA Reflex/end-to-end system latency, and generic "ultra-low latency" marketing. Use the exact tested variant and optimized/expected connection: wired for wired-only mice, proprietary 2.4 GHz receiver for wireless gaming mice, and Bluetooth only for bluetooth-specific sibling keys. If a source gives connection-specific measured sensor values, fill the connection-specific sibling and use the expected connection for this generic key. Firmware, polling rate, Motion Sync/performance modes, and special editions can change the result; return unk rather than copying a sibling/edition or inferring from sensor model/polling rate.

ai_assist.reasoning_note: Only submit a number when a fetched source explicitly gives movement/sensor/motion latency measured in Bluetooth mode for the exact mouse. Accept labels such as "Bluetooth motion latency," "Bluetooth motion delay," "sensor delay over Bluetooth," or a named movement-delay submetric only when the source states the Bluetooth connection was used. Do not use click latency, button latency, average latency, input latency, polling interval, receiver/2.4GHz/wired/HyperSpeed values, or generic Bluetooth latency ranges. Do not copy RTINGS sensor latency unless the product/result page explicitly says the sensor movement test was run over Bluetooth; RTINGS methodology describes sensor latency as movement reported over USB. LTT Labs "Avg. Latency (Bluetooth)" belongs to connection/click latency unless the page explicitly defines it as a sensor/movement test. If the product lacks Bluetooth, or sources only prove Bluetooth support plus a non-Bluetooth sensor/motion value, return unk with a reason. Prefer instrumented lab measurements; manufacturer claims are acceptable only when numeric, product-specific, and connection-mode explicit. Normalize microseconds to ms and round per contract.

ai_assist.reasoning_note: Extract the structural shell shape, not hand compatibility, button handedness, or generic comfort marketing. Use `symmetrical` for mirrored/ambi-style shells even when the mouse is optimized for right-handed users or has side buttons only on the left. Use `ergonomic` for clearly non-mirrored/sculpted right- or left-handed shells; map source phrases such as `asymmetrical`, `asymmetric`, `ergo`, `right-handed ergonomic`, and `left-handed ergonomic` to this value. Use `hybrid` only when a credible shape source explicitly labels the product hybrid or describes an ambi/symmetrical top/front combined with ergonomic/asymmetric side/back shaping. Do not emit `ergonomic` merely because a source says the mouse is comfortable or has an ergonomic design in generic copy, especially when the same source or a stronger source calls the shell symmetrical/ambidextrous. ai_assist.reasoning_note: Use this field for cursor-output smoothing/filtering caused by sensor/MCU averaging, interpolation, ripple/noise filtering, or forced/automatic firmware filtering. Accept direct phrases such as "Smoothing / Filtering: Yes/No," "zero/no smoothing, filtering, or acceleration," and "no smoothing." Return true only when the exact product or exact sensor/firmware evidence says smoothing/filtering/ripple control is applied, forced, automatic, or cannot be disabled. Return false only from explicit zero/no smoothing/filtering evidence or a reputable spec page that lists Smoothing / Filtering = No. Do not infer false from silence. Do not treat "flawless sensor" alone as proof unless the same source defines flawless as no noticeable smoothing/filtering and maps the exact product/sensor. Do not conflate angle snapping/prediction/angle correction, Motion Sync, LOD, polling rate, or marketing adjectives like "smooth glide" with smoothing unless the source explicitly says they average/filter cursor output. For optional toggles or DPI-conditional settings, use true only if smoothing is applied by default/automatically/forced under normal supported use; otherwise return unk.

ai_assist.reasoning_note: Extract the stock primary left/right click switch identity, not the scroll-wheel encoder, side-button switches, hot-swap capability, switch_type, or click lifespan. Prefer exact manufacturer/model or branded technology names (for example, Razer Optical Mouse Switches Gen-3, LIGHTFORCE Hybrid, Kailh GX SPDT, Huano Transparent Blue Shell Pink Dot, ROG Micro Switch II). Treat brand-only names such as Huano, Omron, Kailh, TTC, or generic phrases such as optical/mechanical switches as insufficient for this field unless the product source presents that wording as the switch's marketed component name; those broad facts belong to switch_brand or switch_type. For hot-swappable mice, record the factory-installed main switches; spare/included switch packs are corroboration only, not a union. When sources disagree by edition, revision, colorway, or sibling model, use only evidence tied to the exact product and otherwise return unk.

ai_assist.reasoning_note: Extract the brand credited for the primary left/right click switches as shipped on the exact product/SKU. Prefer sources that name the switch maker or exact switch model. Map unambiguous model/name prefixes to the maker: Omron D2FC/D2LS -> Omron; Kailh GM/GX -> Kailh; Huano Blue Shell/Pink Dot -> Huano; TTC Gold/Dustproof/Nihil -> TTC; Zippy DF3 -> Zippy. For proprietary names such as Razer Optical Mouse Switches, Logitech LIGHTFORCE, ROG Micro Switches, SteelSeries Prestige OM, or Glorious Switches, return the proprietary brand only when no underlying maker is disclosed for the exact product. Do not infer a value from the mouse brand when the source only says mechanical/optical switches, click rating, quiet clicks, or durability. Use main L/R button switches only; ignore side buttons, wheel encoder, scroll switch, and optional replacement/compatible switch packs unless the product ships with that switch by default. For hot-swappable or switch-pack products, record the factory/default switch brand for the exact SKU; if multiple factory switch choices apply and the exact variant is unknown, return unk. Treat aggregator-only values as lower confidence and prefer unk when they conflict with official or professional review evidence.

ai_assist.reasoning_note: Return the URL for the resolved switch component, not a page about the mouse. Use the exact `switch` value and variant/SKU context first. Accept maker component pages, maker datasheets/spec PDFs, support PDFs, official maker technology pages for exact proprietary branded switch generations, or authorized component distributor/supplier pages naming the exact part when no maker source exists. Product pages, reviews, EloShapes/TechPowerUp/spec databases, Amazon/eBay/AliExpress/resale listings, and pages that only mention the switch can help discover identity but are not valid switch_link targets. If sources only prove a generic label such as "optical switches", "Omron", "Huano", or "mechanical switches", or if the component page only covers a broader family that cannot be matched to the exact model/generation, return unk. For hot-swappable or multi-switch bundles, use the default factory-installed main-click switch for the exact SKU/edition; do not union multiple links.

ai_assist.reasoning_note: Extract the factory/default left/right main click switch technology only. Do not use optical sensor type, scroll-wheel encoder/wheel technology, debounce settings, quiet-click marketing, hot-swap/socket compatibility, or optional switch packs. Map ordinary microswitch/mechanical micro switch/SPDT/galvanic wording, or a resolved Omron/Huano/Kailh/TTC/ROG Micro mechanical component, to `mechanical` only when the source or component row supports it. Map optical/IR/light-based/Titan Switch Optical/Razer Optical/Glorious optical/Prestige OM or other infrared-actuated main click switches to `optical`. Map Logitech LIGHTFORCE or explicit hybrid optical-mechanical primary switches to `optical / mechanical`. Map Logitech HITS/SUPERSTRIKE or explicit Haptic Inductive Trigger System main-click actuation to `inductive`. For hot-swappable mice, use the installed factory switch type; if sources only state compatible switch types and not the installed type, return `unk`.

content_types: component maker pages or datasheets when exact switch identity is known

ai_assist.reasoning_note: Count true only when the mouse has a dedicated molded thumb support area: a shelf, scoop, ledge, wing, platform, or extended side rest where the thumb can sit. Accept source phrases such as "thumb rest", "thumb scoop", "thumb shelf", "thumb ledge", "thumb support", or "extended thumb rest" when tied to the exact mouse. Count false when product images or a credible shape/spec source show a normal side wall only, even if the mouse is ergonomic/asymmetrical. Do not treat thumb buttons, a thumb wheel, thumb-operated trackball, side grip texture, side curvature/groove, or generic "thumb control" as proof of a thumb rest by themselves. For negative values, prefer official/PIF images or a shape database with an explicit Thumb rest = No over mere absence of wording.

ui.label: Thumb Rest
ui.tooltip_md: Define 'Thumb Rest' according to contract. If not provable with evidence, output 'unk' with unknown_reason.

ai_assist.reasoning_note: Return true only when the main/top/middle scroll wheel itself has left/right tilt-click inputs or 4-way wheel scrolling. Count source phrases such as "tilt wheel", "tilt-click scroll wheel", "L/R tilt inputs", "left/right tilt", "left-right tilt", "wheel tilt left/right", "scroll wheel tilt", "tilt-shift button", and "4-way scrolling" as true when they refer to the scroll wheel. Do not count a separate thumb wheel, side scroll wheel, horizontal scroll wheel, ergonomic body tilt angle, adjustable/free-spin/infinite/smart-shift wheel mode, mouse tilt gestures, or software-only horizontal scrolling unless the source explicitly says the scroll wheel tilts left/right. For false, prefer an explicit source statement such as "does not feature a tilt-click scroll wheel" or "lacks L/R tilt inputs"; otherwise return unk rather than inferring false from silence. Exact model identity matters because close siblings can differ, e.g. Basilisk V3 vs Basilisk V3 X/Pro, DeathAdder variants, and G502/G502 X variants.

ai_assist.reasoning_note: Extract the mouse device/body weight in grams for the exact requested model/variant. Prefer manufacturer/support specs; use lab-measured default/actual weight when official specs are absent or clearly not a device weight. Treat "weight without cable"/"excluding cable" as the preferred convention; for wired mice, accept official "mouse weight" but do not subtract cable weight yourself. Exclude shipping/package weight, carbon-footprint kg CO2e, chargers/docks, receivers, grip tape/spare feet, and optional add-on weights. For adjustable-weight mice, store the stock/default body weight without optional add-on weights and let adjustable_weight capture the feature. If a source qualifies battery inclusion/exclusion, use the stated spec as-is and do not add/remove battery mass unless a total installed-battery value is directly given. When a family lists size/color/SKU-specific weights, return only the exact variant; do not average or emit a range. Convert oz/lb/kg to g, round per contract, and return unk if only sibling/family/package weight is proven.

ui.label: Weight
ui.tooltip_md: Mouse weight in grams. Convert oz?g when necessary; keep decimals if provided.

ai_assist.reasoning_note: Extract the mouse body's overall/maximum left-to-right width in millimeters for the exact model/SKU. Prefer official product/support/spec tables. If dimensions appear as L×W×H, take W; if a source labels Height/Width/Depth, use the value labeled Width even when the order is unusual. When a source gives multiple physical widths such as front/back, submit the widest body width unless an explicit "overall width" is listed. Do not use grip width, hand-contact width, package/shipping width, receiver/dongle width, cable length, or retailer axis labels that clearly swap length/depth/width. Convert cm/in to mm and round to 1 decimal. If sources conflict, prefer the latest brand-official page for the exact variant; use shape databases, review labs, and retailers only to corroborate or fill gaps. If the catalog row bundles multiple size variants without an exact size, return unk rather than averaging or listing widths.

ui.label: Width
ui.tooltip_md: Overall width in millimeters. Convert inches/cm to mm; require unit when parsing.

ai_assist.reasoning_note: Return true when the exact mouse can charge or power itself wirelessly through inductive/Qi, magnetic-field, proprietary charging-mat, dock-plus-puck/module, or named systems such as Logitech POWERPLAY/POWERPLAY 2/PowerCore, CORSAIR Qi/MM1000, Razer HyperFlux/HyperFlux V2, or Razer Mouse Dock Pro + Wireless Charging Puck. Accessory sold separately still counts true when official compatibility applies to the exact mouse; aftermarket adapters or generic magnetic USB cables do not make the base product true. Do not count ordinary USB charging, replaceable batteries, charging cables, or a contact/pogo-pin cradle as wireless charging unless the source explicitly says inductive/Qi/wireless charging. Phrases like "wireless mouse charging dock" may mean a dock for a wireless mouse, so verify whether charging uses contacts/pins versus a puck/coil. For false, prefer an explicit no/unsupported statement or close official power/charging documentation plus no compatible-wireless-charger listing; otherwise return unk. Match the exact submodel/edition and do not transfer values between siblings.
